objects.name: OPSIGHT — Operator Behavior Monitoring System
objects.description: OPSIGHT is a passive operator behavior monitoring and real-time anomaly detection system for Industrial Control Systems. This identity represents the OPSIGHT CTI engine that produces threat intelligence objects from detected behavioral anomalies correlated with MITRE ATT&CK for ICS techniques.

objects.name: Malicious Insider — Compromised Operator
objects.description: An operator whose credentials have been compromised or who is deliberately acting outside authorized procedures. Behavioral indicators include commands issued outside shift windows, abnormal setpoint changes, and PID parameter modifications without a corresponding maintenance ticket.
goals: Sabotage control process by altering setpoints
Cause physical damage to pipeline infrastructure

objects.name: PIPEDREAM / CHERNOVITE Operator Group
objects.description: Advanced persistent threat group known for targeting ICS/SCADA environments using purpose-built ICS attack frameworks. Capable of interacting directly with Modbus, OPC-UA, and other industrial protocols to manipulate PLCs and safety systems.
goals: Gain persistent access to OT environments
Manipulate physical process via Modbus commands

objects.name: Opportunistic Scanner — Reconnaissance Actor
objects.description: Low-sophistication actor that performs Modbus device enumeration, address scanning, and function code probing against exposed or inadequately segmented ICS networks. Often precedes more targeted exploitation.
goals: Enumerate Modbus slave addresses on the serial bus
Identify valid function codes for targeted exploitation

objects.name: Unauthorized Setpoint Modification via Modbus Write
objects.description: Attacker issues Modbus function code 0x10 (Write Multiple Registers) commands to change the pipeline pressure setpoint to values outside the approved operational range (e.g., SetPoint=90 PSI when normal range is 50-70 PSI). In the Mississippi dataset, this corresponds to the 'IllegalSetpoint' label (198 attack instances). Behavioral indicators include sudden large deltaSetPoint values, setpoint commands from operators without maintenance authorization, and setpoints exceeding process safety limits.

external_references.description: Modify Parameter — Change setpoint or operating parameters to unsafe values.
Unauthorized Command Message — Send unauthorized Modbus write commands.

objects.name: PID Controller Parameter Tampering
objects.description: Attacker modifies PID controller tuning parameters (Gain, Reset, Rate, Deadband, CycleTime) via Modbus 0x10 write commands to destabilize the control loop. Small changes to PID Gain can cause oscillation; zeroing PID Reset removes integral action causing steady-state error. The dataset contains 49 instances labeled 'PIDmodification'. OPSIGHT detects this through the PID_Modification_Rate behavioral feature, which flags non-zero deltaPID values during non-maintenance windows.

external_references.description: Modify Parameter — Alter PID tuning to destabilize control loop.
Manipulation of Control — Tamper with control logic via PID modification.

objects.name: Modbus Address Enumeration Scan
objects.description: Attacker sends Modbus requests to a range of slave addresses (e.g., 0x0F, 0xF0) to discover which devices are present on the serial bus. Typical indicators include commands targeting addresses outside the known device range (normal is 0x04), invalid function codes, and responses with InvalidDataLength. Only 2 instances in the dataset, reflecting the stealthy nature of reconnaissance. OPSIGHT detects address scans through anomalous Address values deviating from the operator baseline.

external_references.description: Remote System Discovery — Enumerate Modbus slave addresses.
Network Service Scanning — Probe for active Modbus services.

objects.name: Modbus Function Code Enumeration
objects.description: Attacker sends requests with various function codes to a known Modbus slave to determine which functions are supported. Responses containing InvalidFunctionCode or exception codes reveal supported functionality. The dataset contains 9 instances. Behavioral indicators include a high Invalid_Command_Rate and function codes outside the operator's normal repertoire (typically 0x03 read and 0x10 write for gas pipeline operations).

external_references.description: Network Service Scanning — Enumerate supported Modbus function codes.

objects.name: Burst Response Injection Attack
objects.description: Attacker injects a rapid burst of spoofed Modbus response packets into the serial bus, overwhelming the HMI with false sensor readings. Characterized by multiple responses arriving with TimeInterval=0 or near-zero, causing the HMI to display fabricated PipelinePSI values. 217 instances in the dataset. OPSIGHT behavioral detection relies on Command_Frequency and Inter_Command_Std anomalies — burst patterns show dramatically compressed inter-command timing.

external_references.description: Spoof Reporting Message — Inject spoofed response frames.
Manipulation of View — Alter displayed process values.

objects.name: Fast-Rate Response Injection
objects.description: A sustained response injection at a rate faster than the normal polling cycle but slower than a full burst. The attacker injects fabricated Modbus responses at a cadence slightly above normal, making detection harder than burst attacks. 225 instances in the dataset. The HMI may display subtly incorrect but plausible-looking sensor values, gradually misleading operators.

external_references.description: Spoof Reporting Message

objects.name: Slow-Rate Response Injection
objects.description: Low-and-slow response injection where spoofed Modbus responses are injected at intervals matching or slightly slower than normal polling, making them blend with legitimate traffic. 306 instances in the dataset — the highest among response injections, reflecting its evasive nature. OPSIGHT detection relies on Process_Command_Correlation anomalies where injected sensor values do not correlate with issued commands.

external_references.description: Spoof Reporting Message
Manipulation of View

objects.name: Negative Value Response Injection
objects.description: Attacker injects responses containing physically impossible negative PipelinePSI readings into the Modbus bus. This can cause HMI alarm logic to malfunction or mislead operators into taking incorrect corrective actions. 119 instances. Trivially detectable by process range validation but may bypass systems that only monitor network-level anomalies.

external_references.description: Spoof Reporting Message — Inject physically impossible values.

objects.name: Setpoint-Targeted Response Injection
objects.description: Attacker injects fabricated Modbus responses that specifically mimic a PLC confirming setpoint write operations that never actually occurred, or that reflect a different setpoint than the one the operator actually commanded. 171 instances. This creates a discrepancy between what the HMI displays and the actual physical state, undermining operator situational awareness.

external_references.description: Spoof Reporting Message
Manipulation of View

objects.name: Single-Packet Response Injection
objects.description: A single spoofed Modbus response packet injected at a precise moment to corrupt one specific reading. The rarest response injection variant (33 instances) and hardest to detect due to minimal footprint. May be used for targeted sensor value corruption during a critical process transition.

external_references.description: Spoof Reporting Message

objects.name: Wave-Pattern Response Injection
objects.description: Attacker injects a sinusoidal or oscillating pattern of spoofed Modbus response values, causing the HMI to display a gradual wave in PipelinePSI readings. 168 instances. This mimics a malfunctioning sensor or an unstable control loop, potentially masking the true attack vector. OPSIGHT detects this through Process_Command_Correlation and deltaPipelinePSI pattern analysis.

external_references.description: Spoof Reporting Message

objects.name: Modbus RTU Denial of Service via Malformed Frame Flooding
objects.description: Attacker floods the Modbus RTU serial bus with malformed frames containing invalid addresses (0x00, 0x06), invalid function codes (0x00), excessively large DataLength (123 bytes), and CRC=0. 873 instances — the most prolific attack in the dataset. This overwhelms the RTU and HMI, causing delayed or lost legitimate command/response cycles. Behavioral indicators include extreme Command_Frequency spikes, a surge in Invalid_Command_Rate, anomalous Inter_Command_Mean near zero, and elevated Pump_State_Change_Rate due to missed commands.

external_references.description: Denial of Service — Flood Modbus bus with malformed frames.
Block Command Message — Legitimate commands cannot reach PLC.

objects.name: Abnormal Setpoint Change Magnitude
objects.description: Modbus 0x10 write command with |deltaSetPoint| > 20 PSI in a single transaction, or SetPoint value outside [40, 75] PSI operational envelope. Observed values in attack data: SetPoint=90 PSI (30 PSI above normal 60 PSI baseline).

objects.name: Unauthorized PID Parameter Change
objects.description: Any non-zero deltaPIDGain, deltaPIDReset, deltaPIDRate, deltaPIDCycleTime, or deltaPIDDeadband detected outside a scheduled maintenance window. PID modifications should only occur during planned calibration events. Observed in dataset: PIDCycleTime changed to 1.16e-10 (near-zero, causing control loop instability).

objects.name: Modbus Command to Non-Existent Slave Address
objects.description: Command or response packets targeting Modbus addresses outside the configured device range. Normal operations target address 0x04. Observed attack addresses: 0x0F, 0xF0, indicating device enumeration. Any address not in the registered device table should trigger a reconnaissance alert.

objects.name: Invalid or Uncommon Modbus Function Code
objects.description: Modbus frame with a function code not used in normal pipeline operations. Normal operations use 0x03 (Read Holding Registers) and 0x10 (Write Multiple Registers). Detection of codes like 0x00, 0x08 (Diagnostics), or proprietary codes indicates reconnaissance or exploitation attempts. Observed in FunctionCodeScan: FunctionCode=0x00 with InvalidFunctionCode flag.

objects.name: Anomalous Burst Command/Response Timing
objects.description: Multiple Modbus frames with TimeInterval=0 or near-zero (< 10 ms) in rapid succession. Based on dataset analysis, normal polling intervals for this gas pipeline range from 500 to 2000 ms. A cluster of 5+ frames arriving faster than this baseline indicates response injection burst or DoS flooding. Correlate with Inter_Command_Std approaching zero and Command_Frequency exceeding 3 standard deviations above the operator's established baseline mean.

objects.name: Malformed Modbus Frame Flood (DoS Signature)
objects.description: Modbus frames with CRC=0, DataLength=123, FunctionCode=0x00, InvalidDataLength=True, and InvalidFunctionCode=True. These characteristics are unique to the DoS flooding attack in the dataset and represent deliberately malformed protocol frames designed to consume bus bandwidth.

objects.name: Unsolicited Response Without Preceding Command
objects.description: A Modbus response packet received on the bus without a matching prior command from the HMI. All response injection attacks (Burst, Fast, Slow, Negative, Setpoint, Single, Wave) share this characteristic. OPSIGHT's Process_Command_Correlation feature detects this by tracking the command-response pairing.

objects.name: Physically Impossible Sensor Reading (Negative PSI)
objects.description: PipelinePSI value reported as negative in a Modbus response. Gas pipeline pressure cannot physically be negative under normal atmospheric conditions. This indicates data injection with fabricated, non-physical values. Immediate critical alert.

objects.name: Abnormal High-Risk Command Ratio
objects.description: Operator session where the ratio of write commands (FunctionCode 0x10) to total commands exceeds 2σ above the operator's baseline. Normal gas pipeline monitoring is predominantly read-heavy (0x03). A sudden spike in write commands may indicate an attacker using compromised credentials to alter process state.

objects.name: Rapid Control Mode Switching
objects.description: Operator toggling between AutoControlMode and OffControlMode multiple times within a session without operational justification. The dataset shows attack commands issued during AutoControlMode with SolenoidControlScheme. Frequent mode changes (> 3 per session) deviate from the baseline and may indicate an attacker probing system response to mode transitions.

objects.name: Elevated Invalid Command Rate
objects.description: A significant increase in Invalid_Command_Rate above the operator's baseline. Invalid commands are those with InvalidDataLength=True or InvalidFunctionCode=True flags set. Normal operations produce near-zero invalid commands. A surge indicates protocol violations from reconnaissance scanning (function code probing, address enumeration) or DoS flooding with malformed frames.

objects.name: Abnormal Inter-Command Timing (Mean/Std)
objects.description: Significant deviation in Inter_Command_Mean or Inter_Command_Std from the operator's established baseline. A drastically reduced Inter_Command_Mean (near zero) combined with near-zero Inter_Command_Std indicates automated or scripted command sequences characteristic of burst attacks, DoS flooding, or tool-based injection. Conversely, extremely high variance suggests erratic behavior potentially from a compromised workstation.

objects.name: Abnormal Pump State Change Rate
objects.description: Elevated Pump_State_Change_Rate beyond the operator's normal operational pattern. The gas pipeline pump should toggle between OnPumpState and OffPumpState at predictable intervals based on setpoint and pressure readings. Frequent unexpected state changes may indicate: (1) an attacker manipulating pump controls, (2) response injection causing the HMI to display false pump states, or (3) a DoS condition causing the control loop to malfunction.

objects.name: Elevated SetPoint Change Rate
objects.description: SetPoint_Change_Rate (or SetPoint_Shock_Event_Rate) exceeding the operator's normal baseline. Setpoint changes in a gas pipeline should be infrequent and deliberate. A rapid series of setpoint modifications (high rate) or large sudden jumps (shock events) indicate either an attacker testing system response, attempting to destabilize pressure control, or probing for process limits. Correlates with deltaSetPoint values in the dataset.

objects.name: Abnormally Low Command Entropy
objects.description: Command_Entropy significantly below the operator's baseline indicates repetitive, predictable command patterns typical of automated attack tools rather than human operators. Normal human operators exhibit varied command sequences with higher entropy. Low entropy combined with high command frequency strongly suggests scripted behavior such as systematic scanning or automated injection attacks.

objects.name: Modbus RTU Scanner Toolkit
objects.description: Generic tooling used to perform Modbus slave address enumeration and function code scanning. Common implementations include smod, mbtget, modbus-cli, and custom Python scripts using pymodbus. Produces the AddressScan and FunctionCodeScan patterns observed in the dataset.

objects.name: Modbus Response Injection Framework
objects.description: A tool capable of crafting and injecting spoofed Modbus RTU response frames onto the serial bus. Supports multiple injection modes: burst, fast, slow, single, wave, negative, and setpoint-targeted. Requires physical or logical access to the serial communication medium.

objects.name: Modbus RTU DoS Flooder
objects.description: Malicious program that generates high volumes of malformed Modbus RTU frames (CRC=0, DataLength=123, FunctionCode=0x00) to saturate the serial bus and deny legitimate command/response communication between HMI and PLC.

objects.name: Enforce Setpoint Range Validation on HMI
objects.description: Configure the HMI and PLC to reject setpoint commands outside the approved operational range [40–75 PSI]. Implement a software-enforced setpoint ceiling/floor at the HMI level and a redundant hardware limit at the PLC. Requires approval from process engineering for range changes.

objects.name: Require Dual Authorization for PID Changes
objects.description: Implement a dual-authorization workflow where PID parameter modifications require both an operator command and a supervisor confirmation through the HMI. PID changes should only be permitted during scheduled maintenance windows flagged in the shift management system.

objects.name: Enforce Modbus Address Whitelisting
objects.description: Configure the Modbus gateway or serial interface to only accept frames addressed to registered slave devices (0x04 for this pipeline). Drop all frames targeting unregistered addresses and log the event for investigation.

objects.name: Restrict Allowed Modbus Function Codes
objects.description: Configure the PLC or an intermediate Modbus firewall to only accept function codes 0x03 (Read) and 0x10 (Write) from the HMI. Block diagnostic (0x08), program (0x0D), and all other function codes. Alert on any blocked attempts.

objects.name: Implement Command-Response Pairing Verification
objects.description: Deploy OPSIGHT's Process_Command_Correlation feature to verify that every Modbus response on the bus corresponds to a prior command. Unsolicited responses trigger immediate investigation. This mitigates all response injection attack variants.

objects.name: Deploy Passive Serial Bus Traffic Monitor
objects.description: Install a passive tap on the Modbus RTU serial bus to capture all traffic without introducing latency. Feed captured data to OPSIGHT's Data Collection Agent for real-time behavioral analysis. Ensure the tap is read-only and cannot inject traffic.

objects.name: Implement Bus-Level Rate Limiting for DoS Prevention
objects.description: Configure a Modbus protocol-aware gateway to enforce rate limits on frames per second. Normal pipeline operations generate approximately 1-2 frames per second. A threshold of 10 frames/second with automatic throttling prevents DoS flooding while allowing burst operational commands.

objects.name: Establish Operator Behavioral Baselines with OPSIGHT
objects.description: Use OPSIGHT's Operator Baseline Engine to build per-operator behavioral profiles across command, session, shift, and operator granularity levels. Baselines are trained on normal operation data and updated periodically. Anomalies are scored using Isolation Forest with LSTM and Autoencoder support models.

objects.name: Gas Pipeline SCADA — Modbus RTU Control Network
objects.description: A gas pipeline pressure regulation system controlled via Modbus RTU over RS-485 serial bus. The system consists of an HMI workstation, a PLC running PID-based pressure control, a solenoid valve, and a pressure transducer. Normal operating pressure is 50-70 PSI.

objects.name: Mississippi Gas Pipeline Dataset — Attack Distribution Summary
objects.content:
Source: Mississippi State University Critical Infrastructure Protection Center / Oak Ridge National Labs gas pipeline Modbus RTU dataset.

COMMAND INJECTION ATTACKS (targeting HMI→PLC commands):
  • IllegalSetpoint: 198 instances — out-of-range setpoint writes
  • PIDmodification: 49 instances — PID tuning parameter tampering
  • AddressScan: 2 instances — slave address enumeration
  • FunctionCodeScan: 9 instances — supported function probing

RESPONSE INJECTION ATTACKS (spoofed PLC→HMI responses):
  • Slow:     306 instances — low-rate stealthy injection
  • Fast:     225 instances — above-normal rate injection
  • Burst:    217 instances — rapid-fire flooding injection
  • Setpoint: 171 instances — setpoint confirmation spoofing
  • Wave:     168 instances — oscillating value injection
  • Negative: 119 instances — impossible negative PSI values
  • Single:    33 instances — precision single-packet injection

DoS ATTACK:
  • Modbus RTU DoS Flooding: 873 instances — malformed frame flood

NORMAL OPERATION:
  • Command baseline: 28,086 clean command records
  • Response baseline: 28,070 clean response records

All 14 CSV files share 27 identical features derived from parsed Modbus RTU frames and include delta-computed fields for temporal change detection.

Opportunistic actor performs reconnaissance scanning.
Opportunistic actor performs reconnaissance scanning.

objects.name: PIPEDREAM ICS Attack Framework Campaign (2022)
objects.description: PIPEDREAM (also known as INCONTROLLER) is a modular ICS-specific attack framework discovered in April 2022 and attributed to the CHERNOVITE threat group. It is the seventh known ICS-specific malware and the most capable ever publicly disclosed. It includes modules targeting Schneider Electric and OMRON PLCs, OPC-UA servers, and Modbus-based control systems. The framework can interact directly with industrial protocols to issue native commands, bypassing HMI-level controls. OPSight addresses this threat by monitoring operator behavior at the HMI workstation level — detecting anomalous command patterns that PIPEDREAM-style automation would produce.
objects.objective: Establish persistent OT access and capability to disrupt or destroy ICS-controlled physical processes

external_references.description: CHERNOVITE Activity Group Targeting Industrial Control Systems
APT Cyber Tools Targeting ICS/SCADA Devices

objects.name: Modbus RTU Protocol — No Authentication
objects.description: The Modbus RTU protocol (IEC 61158) has no built-in authentication mechanism. Any device on the serial bus can issue commands to any slave address without credentials. This allows attackers with physical or logical access to the bus to issue arbitrary read/write commands to PLCs, enabling setpoint modification, PID tampering, and denial of service attacks.

external_references.description: ICS-ALERT-11-343-01: Modbus Protocol Vulnerabilities

objects.name: Modbus RTU Protocol — No Message Integrity or Replay Protection
objects.description: Modbus RTU uses a 16-bit CRC for error detection only — not for cryptographic integrity or replay protection. An attacker can capture legitimate Modbus frames and replay them without modification, or inject spoofed response frames with valid CRC values. This enables response injection attacks where the HMI receives falsified sensor readings while the attacker manipulates the physical process.

external_references.description: Modbus Protocol Security Considerations

objects.name: Modbus RTU Protocol — No Rate Limiting or Frame Validation
objects.description: The Modbus RTU serial bus has no built-in rate limiting or frame validation beyond basic CRC checks. The bus will process any frame regardless of source rate, enabling denial-of-service attacks through malformed frame flooding. A flooder sending frames with CRC=0, invalid data lengths, or undefined function codes can saturate the bus and prevent legitimate HMI-to-PLC communication.

external_references.description: Recommended Practice: Improving Industrial Control System Cybersecurity

objects.name: Implement HMI Alarm Review Enforcement
objects.description: Configure the HMI to require operators to view the alarm screen before acknowledging any active alarm. Log all alarm acknowledgements with reaction time and context flags. Flag acknowledgements under 3 seconds for review. OPSight detects alarm suppression via the ReactionTime_ms and AlarmViewedBefore behavioral columns.

objects.name: Enforce SOP Phase Sequencing and Audit Logging
objects.description: Implement procedural controls requiring operators to complete each SOP phase in sequence. Log all phase transitions with timestamps and operator ID. OPSight detects phase bypass via the Phase and ActionType columns, flagging actions that occur out of expected sequence or at anomalous rates.

objects.name: Deploy Process Safety Interlock Monitoring
objects.description: Monitor all safety interlock activations (ESD, pressure relief, emergency shutdown) and correlate with operator actions in the preceding window. Any safety activation should trigger an automatic review of the preceding operator session. OPSight captures ESD events via the ActionType column.

objects.name: Monitor Process State via Modbus Read Polling
objects.description: Attacker or automated tool continuously polls Modbus holding registers to monitor real-time process state — pipeline pressure, pump status, setpoints, and valve positions — without issuing any write commands. This reconnaissance technique is indistinguishable from normal HMI polling at the network level but manifests as abnormally high read ratios and automated polling patterns with no operator screen interaction.

objects.name: Automated Modbus Data Collection
objects.description: Attacker deploys an automated tool that systematically reads all available Modbus registers across all slave addresses, collecting a complete snapshot of process state for later analysis. This generates high-frequency, low-entropy read traffic with uniform timing that differs significantly from human operator polling patterns. Detectable via command entropy, inter-command timing consistency, and auto-poll ratio anomalies.

objects.name: Brute Force Modbus I/O
objects.description: Attacker sends rapid repeated write commands to force process variables to extreme values, overwhelming the PLC control loop and causing the physical process to enter an unsafe state. Unlike a DoS attack, brute force I/O uses valid Modbus write commands (FC 0x10) at an abnormally high rate, making it harder to distinguish from legitimate operation without behavioral baselining. Detectable via command frequency, write ratio, and setpoint change rate anomalies.

objects.name: Change Operating Mode to Disable Safety Controls
objects.description: Attacker switches the control system from automatic mode to manual or off mode, disabling the PID control loop and removing automatic safety responses to process deviations. In manual mode the system will not automatically correct dangerous pressure or flow conditions. This is detectable via abnormal ControlMode transitions, particularly switching from AutoControlMode to OffControlMode or CascadeControlMode outside of expected SOP phases.

objects.name: Alarm Suppression via HMI Interaction
objects.description: Attacker or compromised operator suppresses safety alarms by acknowledging them at machine speed before human operators can review and respond. Unlike network-level alarm suppression (blocking alarm messages), this technique operates at the HMI level — the alarm is received and displayed but immediately dismissed. Detectable only from HMI behavioral data: ReactionTime_ms under 2 seconds, AlarmViewedBefore=0, and zero dwell time on the alarm screen before acknowledgement.

objects.name: Modify Alarm Threshold Settings
objects.description: Attacker modifies alarm threshold parameters so that dangerous process conditions no longer trigger alerts. By raising pressure alarm thresholds or disabling specific alarm conditions, the attacker can operate the process in an unsafe state without triggering operator notification. This requires write access to PLC configuration registers and is detectable as anomalous Modbus write commands to configuration registers outside normal setpoint ranges.

objects.name: Denial of Control
objects.description: Attacker prevents operators from issuing legitimate control commands by flooding the Modbus serial bus with malformed frames or by exploiting the lack of authentication to lock out the HMI. The operator can see process state but cannot issue commands — the control channel is severed. Detectable as zero write commands combined with high invalid command rate and elevated command frequency from a non-operator source.

objects.name: Denial of View
objects.description: Attacker prevents operators from receiving accurate process readings by injecting spoofed Modbus response packets that override legitimate sensor data. The HMI displays falsified values while the actual physical process may be in a dangerous state. The operator can still issue commands but is operating blind. Detectable via process-command correlation anomalies and physically impossible sensor readings.

objects.name: Loss of Availability
objects.description: The pipeline control system becomes unavailable as a result of a successful denial-of-service attack or malformed frame flooding. The Modbus serial bus is saturated, preventing any legitimate communication between the HMI and PLC. Operators cannot read process state or issue commands. This is the impact outcome of a successful Modbus RTU DoS attack.

objects.name: Loss of Control
objects.description: Operators lose the ability to control the physical pipeline process as a result of unauthorized setpoint modifications, PID parameter tampering, or control mode switching. The process operates outside intended parameters and the control loop no longer responds predictably to operator commands. This is the impact outcome of successful process manipulation attacks.

objects.name: Loss of Safety
objects.description: Safety systems are compromised or bypassed, leaving the physical pipeline process operating without adequate protection against dangerous conditions. This may result from alarm suppression, ESD manipulation, or operating the system in an unsafe mode. Detectable via unexpected ESD activations, alarm acknowledgement anomalies, and control mode changes that disable safety interlocks.

objects.name: Damage to Property
objects.description: Physical damage to pipeline infrastructure results from sustained operation outside safe parameters — extreme pressure, temperature, or flow conditions caused by manipulated setpoints or disabled safety controls. This is the terminal impact outcome of a successful multi-stage ICS attack. OPSight detects the precursor behaviors: setpoint shocks, PID tampering, alarm suppression, and loss of safety events.

objects.name: Loss of Productivity and Revenue
objects.description: Pipeline operations are disrupted, resulting in loss of throughput, unplanned shutdowns, or forced manual operation. This is the business impact outcome of denial-of-service attacks, process manipulation, or operator incapacitation. Even without physical damage, disruption to a gas pipeline results in significant financial and operational loss.

objects.name: Module Firmware / Automated Script Execution on HMI
objects.description: Attacker deploys a persistent automated script or compromised module on the HMI workstation that issues Modbus commands without human interaction. Unlike firmware attacks on PLCs, this targets the HMI software layer — the script runs as a legitimate process and generates commands that appear valid at the network level. Detectable only from HMI behavioral data: zero screen dwell, no alarm/trend context review, near-zero clicks before commands, and unnaturally low action diversity.
